abstract:
This article explores the intricate relationship between human capital migration and the sustainable development of socio-economic systems. By substantiating the evolutionary role and dual nature of migration, the study demonstrates that while migrant influxes can strain social protection frameworks, host countries experiencing high migration flows often exhibit greater socio-economic resilience and actively compete for high-quality human capital. 

The research provides a comprehensive analysis of the quantitative and qualitative characteristics of population movement, identifying key drivers for internal and external displacement within the European Union and Ukraine. It tracks migration intensity and emigration trends in these regions and develops socio-demographic portraits of migrants, specifically comparing data from before and after the onset of full-scale war in Ukraine. Furthermore, the paper evaluates and contrasts the risks, losses, and opportunities associated with human capital shifts, ultimately outlining the consequences of these movements for achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in both donor and recipient nations. Based on these findings, the authors propose a new agenda to better align population displacement policies with the demands of global sustainability.
